title: Bell Correlations and Selection Bias
abstract: Selection artefacts are common in science. A method of selecting samples from a larger population may produce bias, in either direction. It may induce correlations between variables independent in the full population, or mask correlations between variables dependent in the full population. Here we propose a surprising application of these familiar ideas. We argue that they are relevant to puzzling correlations uncovered in quantum theory by John Stewart Bell (Bell 1964). In the light of Bell's work and subsequent experiments it is widely believed that the quantum world is 'nonlocal', in apparent tension with relativity. Many hold that the only alternative is to abandon 'realism', the view that there is an objective world independent of measurement. We propose instead that Bell correlations are selection artefacts, in tension neither with relativity nor realism.
summary: This paper proposes that the apparent nonlocality in quantum mechanics is actually a statistical artifact caused by selection bias. By treating Bell correlations as 'correlator bias,' the author suggests that local realism and special relativity can be preserved without needing to invoke spooky action at a distance.
main_problem: The paper addresses the tension between Bell correlations and the principles of local realism and special relativity, questioning if these correlations necessitate nonlocality.
main_result: The author proposes that Bell correlations are not evidence of fundamental nonlocality but are instead 'selection artefacts' or 'correlator bias' arising from conditioning on a specific variable.
method: The author uses a statistical framework of selection bias (specifically collider bias) and directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to re-interpret Bell's theorem.
model_or_system: The paper analyzes standard Bell-type experiments, including V-shaped (entangled pair preparation) and W-shaped (entanglement swapping) geometries, using classical and quantum toy models of selection bias.
key_observables: Bell correlations (violations of Bell inequalities) and the factorizability of joint probabilities.
important_parameters: The distinction between a 'super-ensemble' (full population) and a 'sub-ensemble' (selected subset) defined by a Correlator.
main_assumptions: The proposal does not provide a physical mechanism for the underlying correlations and relies on the assumption that a randomizer can model the unconstrained super-ensemble.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows WWII bomber survivorship bias; Figures 2 and 3 show DAGs for colliders and common causes; Figure 4 shows a Bell experiment setup; Figure 5 shows Bell states; Figure 6 illustrates entanglement swapping (W-shaped) geometry.
paper_structure: The paper introduces selection bias, applies it to Bell's theorem via 'correlator bias,' develops classical and quantum toy models, extends the argument to entanglement swapping (W-shaped experiments), and discusses implications for time-symmetric causality.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Survivorship bias.3 As we will explain, there are two ways to describe this conclusion. One is to say that the proposal avoids nonlocality, preserving locality without sacrificing realism. The other is to say that Bell nonlocality itself turns out to be a selection artefact. This is a terminological issue, depending on what we choose to mean by ‘nonlocality’ when the new option is in view. We will return to this question below (§4.1), referring to Bell’s own discussion of the term. Either way, the upshot is that Bell correlations are much less puzzling than they have seemed, in tension neither with relativity nor realism. We stress that ‘less puzzling’ does not imply ‘not puzzling...

Figure 3: A common cause The second reason does apply in the familiar, causal world. A well-known property of common causes (see Figure 3) is that their effect on correlations is the opposite of a collider. The correlations they produce disappear if we condition on the common cause – if we select cases in which the common cause variable takes one particular value. The explanation is simple. If K co-varies with L because they both co-vary with a common cause J, then if we remove the variation of J, holding it fixed, we remove the co-variation between K and L. Here’s an example. Figure 5: Bell experiment with four possible initial Bell states. Let’s make this classical toy model a little more sophisticated. The real Bell experiment we had in mind can be performed with any of the four so-called Bell states as the initial preparation. Let’s write these four states as C0, C1, C2, C3, where C0 is the singlet state. (See Figure 5.) For each of these states, there is a probability distribution P(A,B|a,b,Ci) giving the predicted joint probability of outcomes (A,B) given settings (a,b) and initial state Ci.
